title: Categorical vs Numerical Data
concept: Machine learning algorithms can only process numbers, so text labels and categories must be converted into numerical values before training.

exercises.prompt: Warmup: What is it called when testing data accidentally bleeds into training data, creating a false impression of high accuracy?
exercises.explain: Data leakage happens when information from outside the training dataset is used to build the model, leading to overly optimistic results.

cards.title: A computer's native language
cards.body: Computers don't read words the way we do. They calculate. When a model tries to predict if a user will buy a product, it can't directly multiply the word 'Green' by a weight.

cards.title: Numerical vs Categorical
cards.body: Data comes in two main flavors: numerical (quantities like age or price that we can do math on) and categorical (labels or groups like color, city, or membership tier).

cards.title: The barcode scanner
cards.body: Think of a supermarket checkout. The scanner doesn't read 'organic honey crisp apple' to calculate your total; it reads a numerical barcode. ML models need a similar numeric code for every category.

cards.title: House price prediction
cards.body: If you're predicting home prices, square footage is numerical. The neighborhood ('Downtown' or 'Suburbs') is categorical. We must convert the neighborhood names into numbers before the model can run.

cards.title: Numbers aren't always numerical
cards.body: Just because something is a number doesn't mean it's numerical data! A zip code (like 90210) is actually categorical — adding or averaging zip codes makes no mathematical sense.

cards.title: Spotting the difference
cards.body: Let's contrast the two data types:

left.text:
Age, Price, Height
Can do math (average, sum)
Continuous scale

right.text:
Color, Country, Zip Code
Labels and groups
No math allowed directly

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a categorical feature?
exercises.explain: Ice cream flavor is a text label representing groups/categories, making it a categorical feature.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An employee ID number (like 8402) is a numerical feature because it is made of digits.
exercises.explain: False. Employee IDs are labels; adding or averaging them is meaningless. They are categorical features represented by digits.

exercises.sentence: Machine learning models require all inputs to be converted to ___ before training.
exercises.explain: Since ML models are mathematical equations under the hood, they can only process numbers.

exercises.prompt: Which data point is a numerical feature?

options.text: The distance of a commute in miles
The name of the city you commute to
The license plate number of your car

exercises.explain: Commute distance is a continuous quantity you can add, subtract, or average, making it numerical.

exercises.prompt: You are building a model to predict hotel booking cancellations. The columns in your dataset are: room_type ('Deluxe', 'Standard'), nights_stayed (integer), and country ('USA', 'Canada'). Which columns must you convert to numbers?

options.text: None, algorithms can read all of these directly

exercises.explain: Correct! room_type and country are categorical features. They must be encoded as numbers before feeding them to the model, while nights_stayed is already numerical. Next up: how we map ordered categories to numbers!
